coredata.dc:description:
Drying is an energy-intensive pretreatment process for producing bioenergy from biomass through thermo-chemical conversion. Efficient drying is important for the cost and sustainability of bioenergy. The objective of this study is to develop and experimentally validate a local thermal non-equilibrium drying model for biomass packed beds. To inform the model with essential effective parameters, namely, the volumetric heat transfer coefficient (
h
v
) and effective thermal conductivities for solid and gas phases, an experimental facility was designed. The study was carried out on wood pellets and wood chips of various moisture contents. The numerical model was implemented in the Porous Material Analysis Toolbox based on OpenFOAM (PATO). Effective parameter identification was carried out by using the local optimization method NL2SOL. The experimental–numerical inverse analysis showed that reducing moisture from 6.2% to 0.4% increased
h
v
by 20.2% for wood pellets, and from 9.1% to 0.4% increased
h
v
by 11.9% for wood chips, at a Reynolds number of 61 and temperatures below 347 K. Furthermore, two new correlations for the Nusselt number as a function of moisture content have been proposed and validated under the conditions of our experiment:
N u
( M C )

for wood chips. These moisture-dependent correlations extend classical packed bed heat transfer models and can be directly integrated into reactor scale simulations for drying and thermochemical conversion processes.

head.ce:title: Development and experimental validation of a local thermal non-equilibrium drying model for biomass packed beds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Drying is an energy-intensive pretreatment process for producing bioenergy from biomass through thermo-chemical conversion. Efficient drying is important for the cost and sustainability of bioenergy. The objective of this study is to develop and experimentally validate a local thermal non-equilibrium drying model for biomass packed beds. To inform the model with essential effective parameters, namely, the volumetric heat transfer coefficient (
h
v
) and effective thermal conductivities for solid and gas phases, an experimental facility was designed. The study was carried out on wood pellets and wood chips of various moisture contents. The numerical model was implemented in the Porous Material Analysis Toolbox based on OpenFOAM (PATO). Effective parameter identification was carried out by using the local optimization method NL2SOL. The experimental–numerical inverse analysis showed that reducing moisture from 6.2% to 0.4% increased
h
v
by 20.2% for wood pellets, and from 9.1% to 0.4% increased
h
v
by 11.9% for wood chips, at a Reynolds number of 61 and temperatures below 347 K. Furthermore, two new correlations for the Nusselt number as a function of moisture content have been proposed and validated under the conditions of our experiment:
N u
( M C )
p = 2 +
( 0 . 545 − 1 . 2 M C )
R
e
0 . 6
P
r
1 / 3
for wood pellets and
N u
( M C )

( 0 . 524 − 0 . 9 M C )
R
e
0 . 6
P
r
1 / 3
for wood chips. These moisture-dependent correlations extend classical packed bed heat transfer models and can be directly integrated into reactor scale simulations for drying and thermochemical conversion processes.

ce:section.ce:para:
Wood by-products constitute a renewable and clean energy source [1,2]. Processed into chips or compressed pellets [3], they are used as combustibles for residential heating, industrial boilers, and power plants, providing sustainable heat sources [4], or converted in biochar and biofuels by pyrolysis and gasification [5–7]. For these applications, the most common reactors are packed bed and fluidized bed [8], the former is typically used in small-scale laboratory systems, while the latter is used in large-scale industrial systems [5,9,10]. Both reactors involve complex heat and mass transfer processes. Moisture content in wood affects the drying behavior of fuel in reactors. Higher moisture content leads to a longer drying process, delaying the occurrence of subsequent heat-conversion processes, such as pyrolysis and char combustion [11]. Research has shown that the water vapor released during drying can affect the yield and properties of pyrolysis products [12]. In addition, increased moisture content can lead to lower biochar quality, reduced syngas yield, and increased
CO
2
emissions in biochar reactors [13]. Therefore, understanding the thermal conversion behavior during drying is essential for improving process efficiency, as drying can account for up to 15%–25% of the total energy consumption in biomass conversion processes [14,15]. Even a modest improvement in drying performance can lead to significant energy savings at the industrial scale.

The drying process is complex, involving the coupling of heat and mass transfer, and drying kinetics. Various models have been developed at both the particle (micro) and bed (macro) scale [1]. At the bed scale, the wood bed may be seen as a porous medium composed of a solid wood matrix and pores filled with gas [16]. Heat and mass transfer in a packed bed can either be studied under the assumption of local thermal equilibrium (LTE) [17,18] or local thermal non-equilibrium (LTNE) [9,19]. In LTE models, it is assumed that the average temperatures of the solid and gas phases are equal. A single equation is used to model the entire packed bed system. This model is best suited for solids with much higher thermal conductivity and capacity than the fluid, expressed as
ρ
s
c
p , s
≫
ρ
g
c
p , g
[20]. In LTNE models, the temperature difference between the two phases is evaluated by considering two energy conservation equations – one for the solid phase and one for the gas phase – that are coupled through a heat exchangeterm [19,21]. Regardless of whether the LTE or LTNE model is used to investigate the thermal behavior during drying, a common approach is to establish additional equations to describe the drying kinetics. Based on a review of the packed-bed biomass drying and combustion modeling performed by Khodaei et al. [1], three different models have been used to study and analyze the drying kinetics in the published studies: the equilibrium model [3,22], the heat sink model, and the Arrhenius model [1,10]. In the equilibrium model, liquid water in the wood pores is in equilibrium with the surrounding water vapor. The partial pressure of water vapor is determined by the saturation pressure. This model is valid for low-temperature drying [23]. In the heat sink model, the drying zone is assumed to be infinitely thin, and the drying rate is controlled by heat transfer. Since the drying process in this model is assumed to only occur at the boiling temperature, it is suitable for high-temperature drying [24]. The Arrhenius model considers the reaction and thermal time scales, which are valid for various temperatures. In the Arrhenius model, a first-order kinetic reaction rate has been proposed to model the water conservation from liquid to gas in packed-bed biomass particles [25].

Detail models have been presented that couple the energy equation with drying kinetics or the mass and momentum conservation with the energy equation and drying kinetics to describe the drying of particles on a bed scale. Guo et al. [3] used a one-dimensional LTE model without convection term to study the thermal conversion behavior of wood pellets in a packed bed. The equilibrium model was used to calculate drying kinetics. The energy equation neglected evaporation heat loss, which accounted for only 0.82% of the total energy change at a maximum temperature of 55 °C. Bennamoun et al. [22] coupled the mass, momentum (Darcy’s law), and energy equations (LTE model with convection term) with the drying kinetic (equilibrium model) to study the drying of grapes in a packed bed. That study focused on the effects of air properties, such as temperature, velocity, and humidity, on drying. Souza et al. [10] applied an LTNE model to simulate heat and mass transfer in a packed bed dryer. They incorporated constitutive equations for drying kinetics based on the Arrhenius model. The experimental results of the effect of moisture and temperature of the dried material (soybean seeds) were compared with the simulation results, showing good agreement. In these works, the impact of the drying process on the thermophysical properties of the gas phase, i.e., gas thermal conductivity, gas density, and gas dynamic viscosity, has not been addressed. For high-temperature applications at the bed scale, detailed LTE models that account for the temperature dependency of the thermophysical properties have been developed [17]. They consist of models of drying and pyrolysis, and conservation of gas mass, solid mass, species, momentum and energy.

This study develops and validates a detailed LTNE drying model for biomass-packed beds with wood chips and pellets of varying moisture contents. To feed this model, several effective parameters, namely, the volumetric heat transfer coefficient,
h
v
, the effective (solid and gas) thermal conductivities,
k
s,eff
and
k
g,eff
, associated with energy conservation need to be determined [20]. To determine these parameters from experimental measurements, inverse analysis is adopted in this work. Among the various strategies available for inverse problems, optimization-based approaches have shown particular promise in handling nonlinear and coupled systems without requiring simplifying assumptions [26,27]. In this study, a local gradient-based algorithm (NL2SOL), implemented via the Dakota optimization toolkit [28], is employed to minimize the difference between model predictions and transient temperature measurements. Compared to analytical inverse methods that rely on linearization or closed-form derivations and are typically limited to simplified geometries or assumptions, optimization-based inverse analysis offers greater flexibility in handling complex physical models and integrating experimental constraints [26]. While other approaches such as Bayesian inference [29,30] or surrogate-model-based inversion [31] are also used in literature, they often require large datasets or introduce additional uncertainty. The selected optimization algorithm strikes a balance between computational efficiency and accuracy, making it well-suited for estimating thermal parameters in porous biomass-packed beds. However, obtaining accurate values for these parameters becomes challenging in complex geometries, such as packed beds of wood chips or pellets. This is due to factors including geometric configurations [32], namely anisotropy [33] and moisture content [2]. In light of these challenges, the purpose of this study is to develop a detailed local thermal non-equilibrium model, coupled with mass and momentum conservation, to describe heat transfer in biomass-packed beds during the drying process. A laboratory-scale packed bed facility has been constructed to support this modeling effort, with gas temperatures at the inlet and outlet, as well as particle temperatures within the bed, being monitored.

[44]: a major volume of voids in the packed bed (outside the wood particles) and a minor volume of voids inside the wood particles. Guo et al. [3] developed an empirical formula to describe the effective thermal conductivity in the LTE model, accounting for moisture content and porosity under static conditions. . Consequently, any correlations are specific to the particular medium. Thus, for moisture-laden biomass-packed beds, it is necessary to evaluate the dispersion behavior of the gas.

Existing LTNE models at the packed-bed scale typically assume that volumetric heat transfer parameters are independent of moisture content; to date, no study has quantified how moisture affects the volumetric heat transfer coefficient (
h
v
) and the effective thermal conductivity (
k
i,eff
) in biomass beds. The objectives of this study are as follows: (1) construct a laboratory-scale TSBT facility to enable well-controlled drying experiments in biomass-packed beds; (2) determine
h
v
and
k
i,eff
through inverse analysis for wood pellets and wood chips at different moisture contents; (3) validate a detailed LTNE model using transient temperature measurements within the packed bed; (4) develop moisture-dependent Nusselt number correlations for use in bed-scale simulations. These moisture-dependent correlations can enhance the design of biomass dryers and improve the accuracy of CFD models for combustion and gasification in packed beds. The article is organized as shown in the workflow in Fig. 1. In Section 2, the physical properties of wood particles, the experimental facilities, and the testing procedures are presented. In Section 3, the developed numerical inverse analysis method is outlined. This method employs a multi-objective optimization approach to minimize errors between experimental and simulated data. In Section 4, the experimental results are presented, along with the determined effective values of this detailed LTNE model, and the effects of moisture content are analyzed. Conclusions and outlook are discussed in Section 5.

The experimental facility discussed in this section employs a method known as the transient single-blow technique (TSBT) [41]. This method involves using hot gas to heat biomass particles within packed beds, with gas temperatures monitored at both the inlet and outlet. In our work, in addition to recording gas temperatures, the temperatures of selected wood particles within the packed bed are also monitored. This approach provides additional data by capturing solid temperatures at various locations, thereby enhancing the robustness of the inverse analysis.

Two kinds of particles are used in this research: wood pellets and wood chips. The material properties are discussed from two perspectives: one focusing on the intrinsic characteristics of the material, such as dimension, shape, density, and moisture content; and the other on the main features of the packed bed, including the bulk density of the sample and packing porosity. The thermal properties of the material, such as thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity, will be discussed in Section 3. The biomass materials used in this study were tested in their as-received state without any artificial modification of moisture content, in order to ensure industrial relevance of the thermal measurements.

Fig. 2 presents photographs and size distributions of the two biomass types. The wood pellets have a diameter of 6.1 ± 0.1 mm and a length ranging from 5 to 30 mm. The size distribution was determined by measuring the lengths of 400 pellets from each of the three lots using a caliper. A single wood pellet can be approximated as a cylindrical shape. According to Fig. 2(b), the most common length distribution of pellets ranges between 9 and 11 mm. The equivalent diameter of the biomass particles was calculated using shape-specific formulas, following the approach of Pozzobon et al. [49]. For cylindrical wood pellets, the equivalent diameter
d
e p

denote the diameter and average length of the pellets, respectively. The average length of the measured pellets was 10.7 mm, resulting in an equivalent diameter of 7.1 mm. The density of a single wood pellet, determined through multiple measurements, is 1197 kgm−3 with an uncertainty of
± 1 . 9 %
. The moisture content of the pellets, measured using a moisture analyzer (METTLER TOLEDO, HC103), is found to be 6.2% at a room temperature of 25 °C and an ambient humidity of 80%. These pellets were sourced from regular suppliers used in biomass heating applications, and their moisture level reflects typical values encountered in industrial practice. The measurement ensures that the thermal characterization and modeling are representative of realistic usage scenarios. The packing porosity, calculated based on gravimetric analysis, is determined to be 0.43. The bulk density of the pellets is 682.3 kgm−3.

While wood chips vary widely in shapes and sizes, they can be considered roughly as parallelepipeds. Their granulometry ranges (length, height, width) between 2.0 and 30 mm. The equivalent diameter

denote the chip’s length, width, and height, respectively. From 400 measurements, the most frequent equivalent diameter ranges between 6.0 and 7.5 mm, with an average of 7.1 mm. The beech wood chips used in this study were supplied by S.P.P.S. (Argenteuil, France). Although no industrial analysis was performed on the specific batch, the typical elemental composition of beech wood is well-documented. For example, Anca-Couce et al. [50] reported an ultimate analysis of: C 49.5 wt % (d.b.), H 6.5 wt % , N 0.25 wt % , S 0.05 wt % , and O (by difference) 42.9 wt % . Given the relatively low concentrations of nitrogen and sulfur, and since this study focuses on low-temperature drying where no thermal decomposition occurs, these components are not expected to affect the drying process. Although the pellets and chips differ in geometry, this shape-dependent equivalent diameter offers a consistent basis for comparison between the two materials, especially in evaluating bulk-scale characteristics such as porosity, as well as in subsequent modeling. Given that both
d
e p
and
d
e c
were derived from large sample sets (400 particles per material type) and reflect average volume-to-surface ratios, the influence of this approximation on the drying behavior of packed beds is considered negligible.

The density of a single wood chip, determined through multiple measurements, is 552 kgm−3 with an uncertainty of
± 2 . 0 %
. The moisture content was measured and found to be 9.1% (on a dry weight basis) under the same conditions as before: a room temperature of 25 °C and an ambient humidity of 80%. Similar to the pellets, this value reflects the natural moisture level of the beech wood chips supplied by S.P.P.S. (France), a standard biomass provider. No pre-conditioning or artificial wetting was applied. The goal of the study is to capture the thermal behavior of packed beds under operationally relevant conditions, and thus using unaltered industrial materials enhances the applicability of the findings. The packing porosity is 0.47. Table 1 lists the physical properties of both materials. The difference in initial moisture content between pellets and wood chips influences the drying behavior, with higher moisture content generally associated with a slower initial drying rate.

All experiments were conducted in a laboratory-scale packed bed system, which was specifically designed for this study. A schematic diagram of the experimental setup and a detailed view of the test section, including the positions of the thermocouples, are shown in Fig. 3. The experimental setup is composed of several parts, systematically arranged from left to right. On the left side, a hot air blower (BAK Thermoplastic) functions to supply and heat the air. As one moves toward the middle section, there is a test section made of an iron tube surrounded by thermal insulation. This tube, designed to house wood particles, measures 940 mm in length, with an inner diameter of 194 mm, and a thickness of 3 mm. Regarding the particle arrangement, we note that both wood pellets and wood chips are randomly packed in the bed due to manual loading and irregular shapes. Given the large diameter ratio (tube diameter to particle equivalent diameter), the influence of local packing variations is expected to be negligible. Moreover, the adopted volume-averaged modeling approach inherently smooths out small-scale packing differences. Previous numerical studies using CFD-DEM coupling [51,52] have shown that while different packing structures may influence local porosity distributions, their impact on volume-averaged heat transfer properties remains limited.

In practical applications, the diameter ratio, defined as the ratio of the tube diameter to the equivalent particle diameter, is a critical criterion. Previous studies [9,51,52] have suggested that a diameter ratio exceeding 10 is sufficient to minimize wall effects and ensure representative flow conditions within packed beds. In this study, the tube diameter is 194 mm, while the equivalent diameter of the biomass particles is 7.1 mm, resulting in a diameter ratio of 27.3. This ratio satisfies the aforementioned criterion, ensuring that the experimental setup and the associated model are applicable to various biomass sizes, as long as the ratio remains above the threshold. Therefore, it is not necessary to redesign the packed bed for each biomass type, which maintains the economy and generality of the system. On the two sides of the iron tube, two metallic grids are strategically placed to maintain the position of the wood particles. Outside the iron tube, a differential pressure transmitter (EMERSON FISHER ROSEMOUNT) is used to measure pressure drop. On the right side, a thermo-anemometer (VT 110-2014 THERMO-ANEMOMETER) is installed to record the airflow velocity.

To investigate the impact of moisture content on the heat transfer characteristics of wood pellets and wood chips, both materials were subjected to drying in a convection oven at 120 °C for 24 h. Consequently, four types of materials were prepared: moist wood pellets (MC = 6.2%), dry wood pellets (MC = 0.4%), moist wood chips (MC = 9.1%), and dry wood chips (MC = 0.4%). In the experiment procedure, the initial step involved setting the flow rate of the inlet gas. To maintain a controlled variable in the experiment, the inlet gas velocity was kept constant. For both moist and dry wood pellets, the air mass flow rate was set at 5.30 × 10−3 kgs−1, corresponding to a magnitude of the Darcy velocity u (or superficial gas velocity) of 0.149 ms−1 at room temperature (293 K). For moist and dry wood chips, the mass-flow rate was established at 5.10 × 10−3 kgs−1, equating to a Darcy velocity u of 0.143 ms−1. Following the determination of the inlet air mass-flow rate at room temperature, the next step entailed setting the heating power, which was also regulated by the air blower. To prevent pyrolysis of wood samples due to high temperatures during the experiment, the maximum inlet temperature was set to 349 K, thereby focusing on the drying process of the wood material. For each moisture condition, two repeated experiments were conducted under the same room temperature to ensure the repeatability and reliability of the results.

The black points in Fig. 3(b) represent the locations of the thermocouples (type K, 1.5 mm sheath diameter). The temperature measurements include the inlet gas temperature (point 1), the solid temperature inside the test section at six central axis locations (points 2 to 7), and four radial locations (points 8 to 11), as well as the tube’s inside surface temperature at four axis locations (points 12 to 15). The pressure difference was measured between point 16 and point 17. The locations of the probes are provided in Table 2. The thermocouples were placed inside the wood pellets and wood chips, as shown in Fig. 4, to ensure precise measurement of the solid temperature.

The thermocouple voltages are collected at one-second intervals by two Pico Technology TC-08 data loggers and transmitted directly to a personal computer. The dimensions of the tube and the properties of the samples are listed in Table 3. The permeability and Forchheimer coefficient are referenced from previous studies [52]. These two parameters are treated as scalars and will be used in the subsequent modeling section.

Experimental uncertainty of the heat and mass transfer process is mainly introduced by the accuracy of the measurement devices and errors associated with parameter calculations [9]. The uncertainties of the devices and the parameters are listed in Table 4, where the error in temperature measurement arises from the positioning of the thermocouple within the tube. The thermocouple is placed inside the sample, and due to the sample’s stacking, the positioning tolerance is
± 10
mm
. This uncertainty in temperature measurement is quantified through repeated experiments, which have determined an uncertainty value of 1.2% [9].

This section presents the numerical approach. With advancements in optimization algorithms and computing resources, inverse methods have gained popularity as a powerful tool for precise parameter estimation. The first subsection introduces the macroscopic numerical model. The second subsection details the optimization algorithm employed in the inverse analysis to determine the parameters.

The dense solid region (tube) is modeled by using the transient heat conduction equation, which represents energy conservation within a solid. In contrast, modeling the packed bed region, comprising wood particles presents a more complex situation. The detailed local thermal non-equilibrium (LTNE) model incorporates the impact of moisture content on the heat transfer process, considering the gas phase as a mixture of air and water vapor. Although the model structure is derived from a generalized framework that accounts for thermal decomposition processes such as pyrolysis and gasification [17,53], the present study restricts its scope to the drying regime. This simplification is due to the low operating temperatures used in our experiments, which are far below the onset of pyrolysis. The inclusion of only the drying stage ensures a safe and experimentally verifiable setup, particularly given the large scale of the reactor. The modeling framework, however, retains the flexibility to incorporate pyrolytic reactions in future studies. In the present drying model, the
N
s
condensed phases include liquid water and the main structural components of wood, namely cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. These phases are modeled as distinct inert solids, following the decomposition used in pyrolysis-oriented models [17]. Although no chemical reactions are considered in this study, this formulation allows mass redistribution and energy storage to be consistently represented within the solid matrix. The gas phase is modeled as a mixture of
N
g
species (e.g., oxygen, nitrogen, and water vapor), with conservation equations accounting for convective transport and source terms from moisture evaporation. It assumes the reactive material to be rigid while modeling the gas phases as a compressible and perfect mixture of gaseous species. The governing equations applicable to the packed bed region are presented.

In the pyrolysis model, it is recognized that the material under investigation may consist of various phases and sub-phases. For example, the main components of the wood pellets include the wood cell walls (composed of sub-phases of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin) and water [17]. The Arrhenius laws are used to model the kinetics of drying and pyrolysis reactions [1,54]. Since only the drying process is considered here, the rate of drying for liquid water

This coupling follows the standard formulation of local thermal non-equilibrium (LTNE) models [9,17,51], enabling the simulation to capture non-uniform heating effects and phase temperature differences during the drying process. Together, these equations ensure a consistent representation of the interplay between drying kinetics, heat and mass transfer, and porous-medium flow behavior.

), zero-gradient (Neumann) boundary conditions are applied throughout the domain, reflecting a convective-dominated regime and negligible axial diffusion at the inlet and outlet. In the tube region, thermal insulation is assumed at the inlet and outlet via

These boundary conditions collectively reflect the physical configuration of the experimental setup and allow accurate coupling between experimental observations and the LTNE model.

The Porous Material Analysis Toolbox based on Open-FOAM (PATO) [56] was utilized for the computations, adopting a multi-block approach. In this methodology, a different set of equations is solved for each region at every time step, with respective boundary conditions applied, derived from energy balances at the interfaces. Temperature evolution is analyzed at the locations listed in Table 2 and shown in Fig. 6, which also illustrates the multi-block mesh and relevant details. The meshing strategy and independence study are consistent with our earlier work [9]. Within the packed bed region, the pressure equation is solved semi-implicitly, employing first-order schemes in time (using Euler’s method) and space (using Gauss linear). This semi-implicit treatment provides a balance between numerical stability and computational efficiency, especially in porous media systems involving strong source terms and steep thermal or pressure gradients. This approach is also applied to the energy equations (Eqs. (11) and (12)), wherein temperature terms are solved implicitly, while other variables are addressed explicitly. This combination improves stability for the stiff thermal terms without requiring the solution of fully coupled nonlinear systems. In the tube region, only the energy equation (heat conduction) requires solving, with temperature terms again treated implicitly and all other variables explicitly. Equations are solved in sequence, with optimal mesh refinement and time step adjustments to ensure simulation convergence. This sequential strategy has been successfully used in our previous work [9,17], and it enables effective time-step control while maintaining convergence.

The composition of the gas mixture includes nitrogen, oxygen, and water vapor, with the species mass fraction being adjusted by solving the species mass conservation equation (Eq. (7)). For the wood particles, this study makes two assumptions: (1) the thermal conductivity of individual wood particles is considered isotropic and represented by a scalar value, and (2) the thermal conductivity of the solid phase remains constant throughout the experiment, not changing with temperature. A numerical inverse analysis method was implemented to determine the effective parameters by minimizing errors between the experimental measurements and the predicted results. Eleven temperature data sequences, denoted as

The optimization process employs an objective function S , defined as the average of the root mean square relative error. This error is calculated between the measured temperatures and those predicted by the model at ten specified positions (

To minimize S , the Design Analysis Kit for Optimization and Terascale Applications (Dakota) [28] was used. Within Dakota, the Adaptive Nonlinear Least-Squares algorithm (NL2SOL) [59,60], a local optimization method, was employed. NL2SOL is renowned for its ability to rapidly deliver optimal value estimates, facilitated by the quasi-Newton update method it incorporates. The efficiency of NL2SOL is further enhanced by a simplification scheme designed to approximate the Hessian, enabling it to surpass global methods in conducting least square calculations efficiently. An overview of this optimization strategy is shown in Fig. 7, with its application to two-equation model optimizations to be discussed in the subsequent section. Recent studies have also explored complementary strategies, such as genetic algorithms, for optimization in packed-bed systems [61]. These methods may serve as useful complements in future work exploring broader parametric landscapes.

The results of measured and predicted temperatures are discussed in this section. In the first subsection, the detailed LTNE model, as outlined in Eqs. (11) and (12), is considered. The parameters determined for this model include the volumetric heat transfer coefficient
h
v
, the effective solid thermal conductivity
k
s,eff
, and the effective gas thermal conductivity
k
g,eff
. In the second subsection, the pressure, velocity, temperature, volumetric heat transfer coefficient, and gas mixture properties fields predicted by the LTNE model are presented. Comparisons with existing correlations are discussed in the final subsection.

, indicating consistent heating trends across both axial and radial directions in the upper section of the packed bed. The primary parameters influencing the predicted temperatures are the effective solid thermal conductivity (
k
s,eff
), the effective gas thermal conductivity (

g,eff

h
v
). Subsequent sections detail the process for determining these parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of three effective parameters used in the LTNE model
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the model presented in the previous section (Eq. (15)), four parameters require optimization: f ,
c
1
,
c
2
, and
c
3
. Studies have shown that the dispersion terms, represented by
c 3 ⋅ R e ⋅ P r ⋅
k
g
in

g , e f f , ⊥
are smaller [9,58]. Therefore, in what follows, we set the values of
c
3
to zero and focus the optimization on three parameters: f ,
c
1
, and
c
2
.

In the performed simulations, a case involving wood pellets with 6.2% moisture content and a mass flow rate of
5 . 3 × 1
0
− 3
kgs−1 was chosen to illustrate the optimization process and to present the two-dimensional simulation results. Setting the upper and lower bounds for the parameters to be optimized is a key step before starting the optimization, as these bounds can influence the search for an optimal solution. Drawing from the literature, specifically the study by Esence et al. [20], the coefficients f ,
c
1

c

were identified as 1.1, 0.57, and 0.1, respectively, when the Reynolds number is under 8000. Guided by the order of magnitudes from the literature and computational efficiency, the ranges for f ,
c
1

c
2
were determined as 0 to 2, 0 to 2, and 0 to 0.5 respectively. These initial estimates and their respective ranges are provided in Table 7.

After 80 iterations, the local optimization algorithm led to model convergence, with the error term S dropping below 0.005. Fig. 10 illustrates how the three variables ( f ,
c
1

c

) influence S . In part (a) of this figure, the size of each point indicates the value of S , and the color represents different values of f . It is shown that when S is less than 0.005, the values for
c
1
center around 0.79 and for
c
2
around 0.09 (roughly 0.086), with f varying between 0.3 and 1.4. For a focused examination of the error’s relationship with f , values for
c
1
were fixed at 0.79 and for
c
2
at 0.09, only adjusting f . As seen in part (b) of Fig. 10, S decreases and then increases, reaching its minimum at
f = 0 . 47
, with an uncertainty of 5%. This marks the end of the optimization process, establishing the final values for the variables at 0.79 for
c
1
, 0.09 for
c
2
, and 0.47 for f . In conclusion, as S converges to around
5 × 1
0
− 3
, the optimal settings for f ,
c
1
, and
c
2
, along with the associated values of
h

,
k
s , e f f
, and
k
g , e f f , ∥
, are obtained (Table 8).

Regarding the factor f , the value recommended by Wakao is 1.1, however, this suggestion does not consider the shape and moisture content of wood particles. In this study, the values of f for wet and dry wood pellets are determined to be 0.47 ± 0.03 and 0.54 ± 0.03, respectively, corresponding to
h
v
values of 13640 ± 1.5% Wm−3 K−1 and 16391 ± 0.7% Wm−3 K−1. For wet and dry wood chips, the f values are 0.44 ± 0.03 and 0.52 ± 0.03, respectively, with associated
h
v
values of 12455 ± 1.2% Wm−3 K−1 and 13937 ± 0.5% Wm−3 K−1. The heat transfer coefficient for hot air passing through cold wood pellets or chips increases as moisture decreases, with rates of increase of 20.2% for pellets ((16391-13640)/13640) and 11.9% for chips ((13937-12455)/12455), respectively. This phenomenon can be attributed to water evaporation, which induces a blockage effect at the particle scale [62]. The evaporated water forms a vapor layer or condensed droplets that act as thermal insulators, diminishing the direct contact between hot air and the particle surfaces. As the moisture content decreases, (1) the gas thermal conductivity increases, (2) the resistance to mass transfer is reduced, and (3) the temperature gradient between gas and solid phases becomes larger, all of which contribute to a higher effective heat transfer coefficient.

The coefficient
c
1
(Eq. (15)), is generally suggested to represent the solid phase volume fraction
ɛ
s
. Therefore the conventional values of 0.57 (1-0.43) for both wet and dry wood pellets and 0.53 (1–0.47) for wood chips, where 0.43 and 0.47 represent the packing porosity, respectively, as shown in Table 1, regardless of moisture content. However, our optimization process indicates that
c
1
values are 0.79 ± 0.02 for wet and 0.70 ± 0.02 for dry wood pellets, with corresponding effective solid thermal conductivities
k
s , e f f
of 0.224 and 0.174 Wm−1 K−1, respectively. For wood chips, wet and dry samples show
c
1
values of 0.74 ± 0.02 and 0.67 ± 0.02, respectively, with effective conductivities of 0.212 and 0.142 Wm−1 K−1.
k
s , e f f
decreases by 22.3% and 33.0% as moisture content diminishes. In the case of
c
2

c
2
values of 0.09 ± 0.01 for both wet and dry wood pellets, associated with gas effective thermal conductivities in the horizontal flow directions
k
g , e f f , ∥
of 0.128 Wm−1 K−1. The dispersion term of gas thermal conductivity,
k
d i s , ∥

k
g , c o n
. For wet and dry wood chips, the corresponding
k
g , e f f , ∥
values are 0.111 Wm−1 K−1, where
k
d i s , ∥
measures 7.1 times the value of
k
g , c o n
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color maps of the simulation results in the full domain
ce:section.ce:para:
Simulation results for wood pellets with a moisture content of 6.2%, illustrated as color maps, are now detailed. Figs. 11, 12, and 13 present the pressure and velocity fields in the test section and the temperature difference (
T
g
−
T
s
) fields within the packed bed at 4500 s. As shown in Fig. 11, the pressure at the inlet (represented by the red region on the left) is 101440 Pa, while at the outlet (the blue region on the right), it is 101325 Pa. The pressure gradient across the packed bed results from applying the Forchheimer equation (Eq. (8)), which accounts for non-linear drag effects in porous media. In Fig. 12, the velocity magnitude u (
ɛ
g
v
g
) at the inlet (left-side red region) is 0.170 m/s, whereas at the outlet (right-side blue region), it decreases slightly to 0.149 m/s. The velocity is updated dynamically based on both the pressure and gas temperature, as governed by Eq. (8). This occurs because temperature influences the thermophysical properties of the gas mixture, such as density and viscosity, which in turn affect the flow behavior through the packed bed.

In the packed bed region, the maximum difference between the local average gas temperature (
T
g
) and the local average solid temperature (
T
s
) remains below 1.5 K, with most areas exhibiting a temperature difference of around 0.7 K. A larger difference is observed in the top-left corner of the graph, attributed to the strong thermal coupling between the solid temperature within the packed bed and the wall’s surface temperature, which is lower than the gas temperature inside the packed bed.

Fig. 14(a–d) displays the fields of thermophysical gas mixture properties across the entire domain (wood pellets, MC = 6.2%,
t = 4500
s). As the inlet gas temperature reaches 338 K, the values of viscosity, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and gas density at the left inlet are 2.06 kgm−1 s−1, 1052 Jkg−1 K−1, 0.0314 Wm−1 K−1, and 1.01 kgm−3, respectively. For comparison, the values for the mixture gas phase of dried wood pellets (MC = 0.4%,
t = 4500
s) are 2.10 kgm−1 s−1, 1028 Jkg−1 K−1, 0.03 Wm−1 K−1, and 1.05kgm−3 respectively. It shows that, compared to dry wood pellets, the most substantial change in gas properties is the gas thermal conductivity, which increases by 4.7%. To better present the internal moisture removal process, we present the spatial evolution of gas-phase species during the drying of moist wood pellets (MC = 6.2%). Fig. 15 shows the mass fraction fields of nitrogen (
N
2
) and water vapor (
H
2
O
g
) at two characteristic time points: the early stage (
t = 50
s) and the final stage of drying (
t = 4500
s). At
t = 50
s, the gas composition remains close to the initial dry state, with nearly uniform nitrogen distribution (Fig. 15(a)) and negligible water vapor content across the bed (Fig. 15(c)). In contrast, at
t = 4500
s, a clear increase in
H
2
O
g
is observed, particularly in the central region of the bed (Fig. 15(d)), indicating progressive moisture release from the solid matrix. Simultaneously, the
N
2
mass fraction slightly decreases due to the growing presence of evaporated water vapor (Fig. 15(b)). These gas-phase distributions provide a representative visualization of the moisture migration process within the packed bed.

Heat and mass transfer in biomass packed beds of wood chips and pellets with various moisture contents during the drying process is a complex phenomenon. In this study, a novel correlation is developed using the numerical inverse analysis method, taking the equivalent diameter

as the characteristic length. The dimensionless heat transfer correlations are:

Since only two data points for different moisture contents were available for each type of wood particle in our experiments, we assumed a linear relationship to express f as a function of MC.

Fig. 16 presents a comparison of the Nusselt number between the present work and correlation prediction [25,34,63,64]. The black and red dots represent the four data sets for wood pellets and wood chips from Table 8. The green dashed and dotted lines represent Eq. (17) (
N u
( M C )

) for MC values of 0.4% and 6.2%, respectively. Similarly, the red dashed and dotted lines correspond to Eq. (18) (
N u

) for MC values of 0.4% and 9.1%, respectively. The Wakao [34] and Achenbach [63] correlations are used to compare the results obtained in this study. The shaded area indicates a ±20% deviation from Eq. (17) for the case of MC = 0.4%. The ±20% deviation for Eq. (18) at MC = 0.4% is not shown, as the difference between the two equations is negligible, allowing for a clearer figure presentation. The Nusselt number correlations obtained in this study, which account for moisture content, are lower than the classical Wakao correlation [34], with deviations up to 51%, and moderately lower than the Achenbach correlation [63], with deviations around 19% for moist wood chips. The blue downward triangle markers represent experimental data from Peters et al. [25] on the drying process in packed beds of wood cubes, which closely match the conditions of our experiments. For comparison, their drying temperature was 120 °C, with a moisture content of 10%. Mahmoudi et al. [64] also recommend using Achenbach’s correlation for analyzing the drying process in biomass packed beds. Compared to Achenbach’s correlation, our study introduces moisture content as a variable, providing a more accurate representation of Nusselt numbers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations and perspectives for future work
ce:section.ce:para: In future work, we aim to extend the model to higher Reynolds number regimes and broader thermal conditions to assess its robustness under more demanding operating scenarios. The incorporation of more complex geometries and additional biomass types will also be considered to enhance the generalizability of the correlations. Moreover, the effects of moisture-dependent physical properties and dynamic porosity changes will be explored through coupled shrinkage and flow models. These developments are expected to further improve the predictive capabilities of LTNE-based drying simulations and support their application at the reactor scale.

The objective of this work was to develop a detailed LTNE model for biomass packed beds applied to wood chips and pellets of various moisture contents. This model required the determination of several effective parameters. To address this, an experimental facility was designed, utilizing the transient single-blow technique to measure these parameters.

Experiments were conducted using hot air to heat wood pellets (with moisture contents of 0.4% and 6.2%) and wood chips (with moisture contents of 0.4% and 9.1%) at a Reynolds number of around 61. The maximum temperature was set at 349 K to specifically focus on the drying process of the wood particles. Parameter estimations were carried out through numerical inverse analysis. A detailed local thermal non-equilibrium (LTNE) model was employed to investigate heat and mass transfer phenomena. The numerical model was implemented in the PATO software. The local optimization algorithm NL2SOL was employed to minimize the error between measured and predicted temperatures.

The results demonstrate that moisture content influences the volumetric heat transfer coefficient and effective thermal conductivity within the test range. Specifically, the volumetric heat transfer coefficient increased as the moisture content decreased. For wood pellets, it rose from 13640 ± 1.5% to 16391 ± 0.7% Wm−3 K−1 as moisture content dropped from 6.2% to 0.4%. Similarly, for wood chips, it increased from 12455 ± 1.2% to 13937 ± 0.5% Wm−3 K−1 when moisture content decreased from 9.1% to 0.4%. As one may expect, water evaporation contributes to a blockage effect at the particle scale, impacting the heat transfer processes.

Two new correlations for the Nusselt number have been proposed:
N u
( M C )
p = 2 +
( 0 . 545 − 1 . 2 M C )
R
e
0 . 6
P
r
1 / 3
for wood pellets and
N u
( M C )
c = 2 +
( 0 . 524 − 0 . 9 M C )
R
e
0 . 6
P
r
1 / 3
for wood chips. These correlations account for moisture induced changes in the dimensionless numbers and have been validated at specific moisture contents of 0.4% and 6.2% for wood pellets, and 0.4% and 9.1% for wood chips, at a Reynolds number of around 61, air temperature of 349 K, and particle diameters between 6–9 mm. Their application to other flow regimes or biomass types may require recalibration. These moisture-dependent correlations extend classical packed bed heat transfer models and can be directly implemented in reactor-scale simulations. The results also highlight the importance of accounting for degassing in pyrolysis applications, emphasizing the need to continue this work beyond the drying stage.
